title: The 25 Best Movies of the Century: No. 24 - ‘The Handmaiden’
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins put Park Chan-wook's The Handmaiden at No. 24 on their 25-for-25 list, picking it over Oldboy. They cover why: the Sarah Waters adaptation moved from Victorian England to 1930s colonial Korea, the three-act shifting-perspective structure, the argument over whether its sex scenes are exploitative or the film's whole point, its odd Amazon provenance, and where it sits in the Korean New Wave on the road to Parasite. Ends with a recommended-if-you-like slate.

films.title: The Handmaiden
films.note: The pick, at No. 24. Park Chan-wook's 2016 thriller, co-written with Chung Seo-kyung, adapting Sarah Waters's 2002 novel Fingersmith but moving it from Victorian England to Korea under 1930s Japanese colonial rule. A Korean conman and an orphaned pickpocket (Kim Tae-ri, in her feature debut) plot to swindle a Japanese heiress, Hideko (Kim Min-hee); the con keeps turning over across three acts told from shifting perspectives. Sean and Amanda say they went back and forth with Oldboy before landing here, calling it the culmination of Park's craft, an erotic thriller of a kind the U.S. abandoned, and probably his best. Much of the talk is the exploitation-vs.-liberation debate over the sex scenes, which they argue are earned by the character's porn-saturated upbringing and by the film's ending, where the two women escape the men entirely.

films.title: Oldboy
films.note: Park's American breakthrough and the Handmaiden's rival for this slot. The hosts grant it's the more historically important film (the hallway fight, the introduction of the Korean New Wave to the U.S.), but say The Handmaiden is the one they actually love.

films.title: Parasite
films.note: The conclusive moment of the Korean New Wave, a few years after this; the hosts ask whether The Handmaiden helped clear the American path Parasite eventually took to Best Picture, and decide not quite.

films.title: The Age of Shadows
films.note: Korea's actual 2016 Oscar submission over The Handmaiden. The hosts read the snub as the country steering away from something this sexually explicit. It wasn't nominated.

films.title: Snowpiercer
films.note: Cited as the counterexample to The Handmaiden's reach: a Weinstein release with a mostly English-language, American cast, where The Handmaiden was the more widely distributed straight-Korean film.

films.title: The Housemaid
films.note: Kim Ki-young's 1960 film about a young woman who joins a wealthy household and detonates it from within, made during a brief lifting of Korean censorship. Scorsese and Bong have both championed it; the hosts hear it as the clear ancestor of this movie, down to the title.

films.title: Stranger by the Lake
films.note: One of the European and Asian erotic thrillers the hosts say kept the genre alive after Hollywood dropped it.

films.title: Blue Is the Warmest Color
films.note: The comparison point Sean returns to: another film accused of exploitation and unrealistic sex, but gritty and realistic where The Handmaiden is a designed, over-the-top period piece.

films.title: Black Book
films.note: Named in the run of lurid-but-classy thrillers Europe and Asia kept making.

films.title: The Skin I Live In
films.note: Another of the persisting erotic thrillers cited, alongside the work of Luca Guadagnino.

films.title: M
films.note: Fritz Lang's film, where Sean starts the lineage of the sex-and-power thriller that runs through Hitchcock and lands on Park.

films.title: Michael Clayton
films.note: Running gag about what's buried on Amazon Prime Video; Sean keeps needing to note where you can actually stream these movies.

films.title: Manchester by the Sea
films.note: Part of Amazon's 2016 art-house spree under Ted Hope, the same slate that brought The Handmaiden to U.S. audiences.

films.title: Paterson
films.note: Jim Jarmusch's film, another Amazon 2016 pickup and also in that year's Cannes lineup.

films.title: Wiener-Dog
films.note: Todd Solondz's film, cited among the filmmakers Amazon kept working that year.

films.title: Love & Friendship
films.note: Whit Stillman's Austen adaptation, which Sean flags as an Amanda favorite, on the same Amazon slate.

films.title: The Neon Demon
films.note: Nicolas Winding Refn's film, an Amazon 2016 pickup that also screened at Cannes that year.

films.title: Café Society
films.note: Woody Allen's film, rounding out the Amazon 2016 acquisitions the hosts list.

films.title: I, Daniel Blake
films.note: Ken Loach's Palme d'Or winner at the 2016 Cannes where The Handmaiden played and went home empty, Loach's second Palme.

films.title: The Salesman
films.note: Asghar Farhadi's Best International Feature winner from that same Cannes, his second after A Separation.

films.title: A Separation
films.note: Farhadi's earlier foreign-language Oscar winner, the reference point for The Salesman's win.

films.title: American Honey
films.note: Named in the rundown of that year's Cannes lineup.

films.title: Elle
films.note: Paul Verhoeven's Cannes 2016 film, which Amanda calls a perfect double feature with The Handmaiden.

films.title: Personal Shopper
films.note: Another title from the 2016 Cannes slate the hosts reel off.

films.title: Toni Erdmann
films.note: Also on the Cannes 2016 list Sean runs through.

films.title: Loving
films.note: Jeff Nichols's film, the last title in the Cannes 2016 rundown.

films.title: Thief
films.note: Sean's Michael Mann comparison: through this movie Park was, to him, still pre-Last-of-the-Mohicans Mann, a master of one grounded, grimy thing. Thief is the archetype.

films.title: Manhunter
films.note: Cited alongside Thief as the hard-bitten early-Mann mode Park had been working in until The Handmaiden bumped him up a tier.

films.title: The Keep
films.note: The exception Sean flags in early Mann's otherwise grounded run.

films.title: The Last of the Mohicans
films.note: The Mann film that marks the leap in Sean's analogy, the point where a filmmaker known for one thing opens up, as Park does here.

films.title: Vertigo
films.note: In the closing recommended-if-you-like slate, and, per Park, the number-one influence on the film. Sean says it changed Park's life.

films.title: Rebecca
films.note: Recommended alongside Vertigo for the Hitchcock strain, with a Mrs. Danvers figure and a house full of illusions.

films.title: Gaslight
films.note: The recommended starting point: the Charles Boyer film that gave the language 'gaslighting,' which the con here runs on.

films.title: Diabolique
films.note: Henri-Georges Clouzot's thriller (Sean says 'Diabolik'), recommended for the twist-driven deception; he notes the bad '90s American remake.

films.title: Bound
films.note: The Wachowskis' lesbian crime drama, recommended for the obvious throughline.

films.title: Wild Things
films.note: The erotic thriller Amanda pulls as the closest youth-era match, too classy for the Adrian Lyne suburban dramas but just as explicit.

films.title: Eyes Wide Shut
films.note: On the recommended slate for the sex-and-power register.

films.title: Gone Girl
films.note: Recommended for the marriage-as-con machinery, though the hosts note it lacks the film's third-woman geometry.

films.title: The Favourite
films.note: Yorgos Lanthimos's film, the closer on the slate, another European working in this mode as America stopped.

films.title: The Town
films.note: In the sign-off: the Ben Affleck film Sean would buy tickets to on the Rewatchables Boston screening tour.

films.title: The Friends of Eddie Coyle
films.note: The other Boston screening Sean mentions in the outro, which he'll be flying and miss.

Books: The Plum in the Golden Vase
Art: The Dream of the Fisherman's Wife
